topics.title: Memory Management — Stack vs Heap
topics.description: Java manages memory through two primary runtime areas: the Stack (per-thread, LIFO, fast, stores frames with local variables and references) and the Heap (shared, GC-managed, stores all objects and arrays). Understanding their behavior is essential for diagnosing StackOverflowError, OutOfMemoryError, memory leaks, and designing memory-efficient code.
topics.story: Imagine a busy restaurant. The stack is your waiter's notepad — they jot down each table's order (stack frame), serve it, and tear out the page when done. It's fast, local, and self-cleaning. The heap is the kitchen's storage room — all the ingredients and prepared dishes (objects) sit there. The notepad pages can have directions like 'grab the pasta from shelf 3' (a reference). When a dish is no longer referenced by any active order, the head chef (GC) eventually throws it out. If the notepad fills up (too deep recursion) — StackOverflowError. If the storage room fills up — OutOfMemoryError.
topics.analogy: Stack = a tray of sticky notes for the current task — LIFO, auto-cleaned when done, fixed small size. Heap = a warehouse — vast, shared by all tasks, things only leave when nobody needs them (GC). References on the sticky notes point to boxes in the warehouse. Throw away the sticky note (method returns) and the warehouse boxes become orphans — GC collects them eventually.
topics.memoryAnchor: STACK: Small, Fast, Frames, Primitives+References, StackOverflowError. HEAP: Large, Shared, Objects+Arrays, GC-managed, OutOfMemoryError. Rule: 'new' keyword → always heap. Local primitive → always stack. Mnemonic: 'Stack of Plates (LIFO), Heap of Toys (scattered, GC cleans up)'.
topics.firstPrinciples: CPUs have registers and memory. Stack grows/shrinks using a single stack pointer (push/pop = 1 instruction). Heap uses dynamic allocators (malloc/GC). Java chose: stack for call frames (predictable lifecycle), heap for objects (unpredictable lifecycle). GC exists because manually freeing heap memory (like C's free()) causes bugs — use-after-free, double-free. Trade-off: GC adds pause overhead but eliminates a whole class of memory bugs.
topics.bruteForce: Without stack/heap separation: all variables on the heap. Every int, double, boolean object-allocated. Massive overhead: allocation cost, GC pressure, cache misses. JVM actually does escape analysis to avoid this — objects that don't 'escape' a method can be stack-allocated even if declared on heap (JIT optimization).
topics.optimizedApproach: JVM optimizations: (1) Escape Analysis — if an object never leaves the method, allocate on stack. (2) Scalar Replacement — break object into its fields. (3) TLAB (Thread-Local Allocation Buffer) — each thread has a private slice of heap for fast bump-pointer allocation. (4) Generational GC — most objects die young (Young Gen), few survive to Old Gen.
topics.whenToUse: Stack knowledge matters for: sizing -Xss for deep recursion, diagnosing StackOverflowError (infinite recursion), understanding why large arrays must be on heap (stack is small, typically 512KB-1MB). Heap knowledge matters for: sizing -Xmx for large data, diagnosing OutOfMemoryError, hunting memory leaks (profilers like JVisualVM show heap snapshots), understanding GC pauses in latency-sensitive systems.

examples.title: Stack Frame Lifecycle — Method Call Chain
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":14,"lineCode":"int base = 5;","variables":{"base":5},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"base stored in main()'s stack frame as a primitive int"},{"line":15,"lineCode":"int result = multiply(base, 3);","variables":{"base":5},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"New stack frame pushed for multiply(). base's VALUE (5) copied into x parameter"},{"line":8,"lineCode":"int doubled = add(x, x);","variables":{"x":5,"factor":3},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Another frame pushed for add(). Stack now has 3 frames"},{"line":3,"lineCode":"int result = a + b;","variables":{"a":5,"b":5,"result":10},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"result=10 stored in add()'s frame (top of stack)"},{"line":4,"lineCode":"return result;","variables":{"result":10},"phase":"RETURN","annotation":"add()'s frame popped. Return value 10 transferred to multiply()'s 'doubled'"},{"line":9,"lineCode":"int product = doubled * factor;","variables":{"doubled":10,"factor":3,"product":30},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"product=30 in multiply()'s frame"},{"line":10,"lineCode":"return product;","variables":{"product":30},"phase":"RETURN","annotation":"multiply()'s frame popped. 30 transferred to main()'s 'result'"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph call_sequence
      A[main() called] --> B[multiply() frame pushed]
      B --> C[add() frame pushed]
      C --> D[add() returns, frame popped]
      D --> E[multiply() continues, frame popped]
      E --> F[main() continues]
    end
    subgraph stack_at_peak
      F3[Frame 3: add() — a=5,b=5,result=10]
      F2[Frame 2: multiply() — x=5,factor=3]
      F1[Frame 1: main() — base=5]
      F3 -->|on top of| F2
      F2 -->|on top of| F1
    end
examples.pseudocode:
CALL method(args):
  allocate new frame on stack
  copy arg values into frame parameters
  execute method body
  RETURN:
    pop frame
    copy return value to caller's frame
examples.explanation: Each method call pushes a new stack frame containing: local variables, method parameters, return address, and reference to outer frame. Frames are pushed/popped in strict LIFO order — hence the name 'call stack'. This structure enables Java's deterministic variable lifecycle: all locals are guaranteed gone when the method returns.
examples.realWorldUse: Stack traces in exceptions are the call stack at the moment of the error — each line is one frame. In production debugging (Datadog, Splunk), you see the exact chain of method calls that led to the error. Thread dumps capture all thread stacks simultaneously — critical for diagnosing deadlocks and high-CPU issues.

examples.title: Heap Allocation — Object Lifecycle
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":9,"lineCode":"Config c = new Config(\"localhost\", 8080);","variables":{"c":"ref→Heap@0x200"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"new Config(...) allocates on heap at 0x200. 'c' is a reference in createConfig()'s stack frame"},{"line":10,"lineCode":"return c;","variables":{"c":"ref→Heap@0x200"},"phase":"RETURN","annotation":"The reference value (0x200) returned to caller. createConfig() frame popped — but object at 0x200 stays alive because reference transferred"},{"line":14,"lineCode":"Config cfg = createConfig();","variables":{"cfg":"ref→Heap@0x200"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"cfg in main()'s frame now holds the reference 0x200. Object stays reachable"},{"line":17,"lineCode":"cfg = null;","variables":{"cfg":"null"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"cfg now holds null. Object at 0x200 has NO more references — GC eligible"},{"line":21,"lineCode":"Config temp = new Config(\"192.168.1.1\", 9090);","variables":{"temp":"ref→Heap@0x300"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"new Config at 0x300. Lives only within the block scope"},{"line":23,"lineCode":"} // block ends","variables":{},"phase":"SCOPE_END","annotation":"temp goes out of scope. Config@0x300 now unreachable — eligible for GC"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph Stack
      SF1[main() frame: cfg → 0x200]
      SF2[createConfig() frame: c → 0x200]
    end
    subgraph Heap
      H1[0x200: Config host=localhost port=8080]
      H2[0x300: Config temp — GC eligible after block]
    end
    SF2 -->|during createConfig| H1
    SF1 -->|after return| H1
    H1 -->|cfg=null → unreachable| GC[GC eligible]
    H2 -->|block ends → unreachable| GC
examples.pseudocode:
new Obj() → allocates on heap
Object alive while at least 1 strong reference
Object dies when 0 strong references
GC collects dead objects asynchronously
Object can outlive the method that created it
  (if reference returned/stored elsewhere)
examples.explanation: Objects on the heap outlive the stack frame that created them, as long as a reference exists elsewhere. This is what enables global state, caches, and long-lived services. GC traces from roots (stack references, static fields) and collects anything unreachable. The key insight: object lifetime is determined by references, not by scope.
examples.realWorldUse: Memory leaks happen when objects are unintentionally kept reachable. Common patterns: (1) static collections that grow without bound (static Map<K,V> cache), (2) event listener callbacks that hold a reference to a widget after the widget is 'closed', (3) ThreadLocal not removed — the thread pool thread keeps the ThreadLocal alive forever. Tools: Eclipse MAT, YourKit heap analyzer, jmap -histo.

examples.title: StackOverflowError — Infinite Recursion
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":4,"lineCode":"static int infiniteCount(int n) {","variables":{"n":0},"phase":"ENTER","annotation":"Frame 1: n=0. New stack frame pushed"},{"line":5,"lineCode":"return infiniteCount(n + 1);","variables":{"n":1},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Frame 2 pushed: n=1. No base case — will push frames until stack is full"},{"line":5,"lineCode":"return infiniteCount(n + 1);","variables":{"n":5000},"phase":"ERROR","annotation":"Frame ~5000: stack is full! JVM throws StackOverflowError"},{"line":9,"lineCode":"if (n <= 0) return 0;","variables":{"n":0},"phase":"BASE_CASE","annotation":"Correct recursion: base case terminates the chain and pops all frames"},{"line":16,"lineCode":"return maxDepth(current + 1);","variables":{"current":6000},"phase":"ERROR","annotation":"Stack fills up. StackOverflowError is a Throwable — can be caught to measure depth"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[infiniteCount(0)] --> B[infiniteCount(1)]
    B --> C[infiniteCount(2)]
    C --> D[...]
    D --> E[infiniteCount(5000)]
    E -->|Stack Full!| F[StackOverflowError]

    G[countdown(10)] --> H[countdown(9)]
    H --> I[...]
    I --> J[countdown(0)]
    J -->|base case: return 0| K[frames pop back up]
    K --> L[returns 10]
examples.pseudocode:
Each recursive call:
  push new frame (~100-500 bytes)
  JVM checks: stack size > -Xss limit?
    yes → throw StackOverflowError
    no  → continue

Fix StackOverflowError:
  1. Add/fix base case
  2. Convert to iterative (stack simulation)
  3. Increase -Xss (last resort)
examples.explanation: Each stack frame consumes memory (local variables, return address, frame pointer). Default JVM stack size is 512KB-1MB per thread. With each frame consuming ~200 bytes, that's ~2000-5000 frames before StackOverflowError. Solutions: (1) always have a base case, (2) use tail recursion (Java doesn't optimize TCO, but manual iteration does), (3) use explicit Stack<> data structure for deep algorithms.
examples.realWorldUse: StackOverflowError in production usually means: (1) accidentally infinite recursion (forgot base case, or base case never reached due to wrong condition), (2) mutual recursion (A calls B calls A), (3) deep object graphs (a serialization/toString that follows all references). In JSON serialization (Jackson), circular references cause StackOverflowError — fix: @JsonIgnore or @JsonManagedReference/@JsonBackReference.

examples.title: Heap Generations and GC Pressure
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":5,"lineCode":"String result = \"\";","variables":{"result":"ref→Heap@0x001"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Empty string on heap"},{"line":7,"lineCode":"result = result + w + \",\";","variables":{"result":"ref→Heap@0x002"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Iteration 1: new String \"word0,\" created at 0x002. Old string 0x001 becomes garbage"},{"line":7,"lineCode":"result = result + w + \",\";","variables":{"result":"ref→Heap@0x003"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Iteration 2: new String at 0x003. 0x002 becomes garbage. N iterations = N garbage Strings"},{"line":13,"lineCode":"StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();","variables":{"sb":"ref→Heap@0x100"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"1 StringBuilder allocated"},{"line":15,"lineCode":"sb.append(w).append(\",\");","variables":{"sb":"ref→Heap@0x100"},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"Same StringBuilder 0x100 grows in-place (doubles buffer when needed). No new objects created"},{"line":17,"lineCode":"return sb.toString();","variables":{},"phase":"RETURN","annotation":"1 final String created from StringBuilder's buffer. Total heap allocations: 2 vs N+1"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph BAD_n10000
      B1[iter 0: String 'word0,'] -->|garbage| GC1
      B2[iter 1: String 'word0,word1,'] -->|garbage| GC1
      B3[...10000 objects...] -->|garbage| GC1
      GC1[GC runs repeatedly]
    end
    subgraph GOOD
      G1[StringBuilder created]
      G1 -->|append 10000 times, same object| G2[toString: 1 String]
      G2 --> G3[2 heap allocations total]
    end
examples.pseudocode:
BAD: String concat in loop
  each '+' creates new String
  O(n) allocations, O(n²) total chars copied

GOOD: StringBuilder
  1 allocation (doubles buffer when needed)
  O(n) total chars copied
  1 final toString() allocation
examples.explanation: String + in a loop creates O(n) intermediate String objects that are immediately garbage. This floods the Young Generation of the heap, triggering frequent Minor GC pauses. StringBuilder maintains a char[] buffer that doubles when full — amortized O(1) per append. The javac compiler optimizes single-line String + expressions to StringBuilder, but NOT across loop iterations.
examples.realWorldUse: A real production incident: a report generator concatenating 10,000 records with String + caused GC pauses every 100ms, making the service appear unresponsive. Fix: StringBuilder reduced allocations by 10,000x. Always use StringBuilder (or String.join, String.format, or Collectors.joining()) for multi-piece String assembly.

examples.title: Memory Leak Pattern — Static Cache Holding References
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":9,"lineCode":"cache.put(key, new byte[1024 * 1024]);","variables":{"cacheSize":1},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"1 MB array allocated on heap. Stored in static cache — will NEVER be GC'd as long as cache lives"},{"line":9,"lineCode":"cache.put(key, new byte[1024 * 1024]);","variables":{"cacheSize":1000},"phase":"MUTATE","annotation":"After 1000 requests: 1 GB allocated! Static field holds strong references — GC cannot collect"},{"line":16,"lineCode":"return size() > 100;","variables":{"cacheSize":100},"phase":"EVICT","annotation":"LinkedHashMap with LRU eviction: when size > 100, removes eldest (least-recently-used) entry"},{"line":22,"lineCode":"static Map<String, byte[]> weakCache = new WeakHashMap<>();","variables":{},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"WeakHashMap holds keys with weak references — GC can collect entries when keys have no strong reference elsewhere"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph LEAK
      R1[Request key1] -->|put 1MB| C1[static cache]
      R2[Request key2] -->|put 1MB| C1
      R3[...10000 requests...] -->|10 GB| C1
      C1 -->|static ref = GC Root| NO_GC[Never collected!]
    end
    subgraph FIX
      BCache[LinkedHashMap LRU] -->|size > 100: evict eldest| Evict
      WCache[WeakHashMap] -->|key GC'd elsewhere| AutoEvict
      Caffeine[Caffeine.maximumSize(100)] -->|W-TinyLFU eviction| Smart
    end
examples.pseudocode:
MEMORY LEAK: static collection + strong references
  static Map cache
  cache.put(key, largeObject) // strong ref
  GC cannot collect → heap fills → OOM

FIX A: bounded LinkedHashMap (LRU eviction)
FIX B: WeakHashMap (auto-evict on GC pressure)
FIX C: Caffeine/Guava cache (time+size-based eviction)
examples.explanation: The classic Java memory leak: a static field holding strong references to objects. Static fields are GC roots — everything they reference is permanently alive. Growing a static collection without eviction eventually fills the Old Generation heap, triggering Full GC pauses, then OutOfMemoryError. WeakHashMap uses weak references for keys — when the key object has no other strong reference, GC collects both key and value.
examples.realWorldUse: Production memory leaks analyzed by Netflix, Twitter engineering blogs: (1) HttpSession caches that store large objects — fix: set maxInactiveInterval. (2) ClassLoader leaks in OSGi/hot-reload systems — class metadata held by old ClassLoader. (3) JDBC Connection held in ThreadLocal not cleared — connection pool exhaustion. Tools: jmap -dump + Eclipse MAT 'dominator tree' shows which object graph holds the most memory.

problems.title: Classify Variables — Stack or Heap
problems.hint1: Static fields live in the Method Area (Metaspace). Instance fields live on the heap inside the object.
problems.hint2: Local primitive variables live on the stack. Local reference variables (the variable itself) live on the stack.
problems.hint3: Objects created with 'new' and String literals/objects live on the heap.

testCases.output: {A_counter=method-area, B_instanceField=heap, C_local=stack-primitive, D_msg_var=stack-ref, E_String_obj=heap, F_arr_var=stack-ref, G_array_obj=heap}

problems.editorial: Static fields → Method Area (Metaspace). Instance fields → heap (stored inside the object). Local primitives → stack (directly in frame). Local reference variables → stack (hold addresses). Actual objects → heap. String literals → String Pool (subset of heap since Java 7).

problems.title: Predict the Error Type
problems.description:
For each scenario below, predict whether it throws StackOverflowError, OutOfMemoryError, or runs fine:

A) `void infinite() { infinite(); }` called once
B) `new byte[Integer.MAX_VALUE]` — allocate ~2GB array
C) `countdown(1000)` where countdown has a proper base case at 0
D) `for(;;) list.add(new byte[1024])` — unbounded list growth

Implement `predict()` returning String[] of A,B,C,D outcomes.
problems.hint1: Infinite recursion = StackOverflowError (stack frames fill up).
problems.hint2: Allocating more memory than heap allows = OutOfMemoryError.
problems.hint3: Bounded recursion and bounded allocation = no error.

testCases.output:
A: StackOverflowError
B: OutOfMemoryError
C: Runs fine
D: OutOfMemoryError

problems.editorial: StackOverflowError: stack size exhausted by call frames. OutOfMemoryError: heap size exhausted by objects. C: 1000 recursion depth is well within default stack limits (~5000+). D: unbounded list growth will exhaust -Xmx heap.

problems.title: Count Heap Allocations
problems.description:
Count how many distinct objects are allocated on the heap in this snippet:

```java
String a = "hello";            // (1)
String b = "hello";            // (2)
String c = new String("hello"); // (3)
int[] arr = new int[]{1,2,3};  // (4)
Integer x = 200;               // (5) — autoboxing
Integer y = 100;               // (6) — autoboxing with cache
Integer z = 100;               // (7) — same cached Integer
```

Return the count of distinct heap objects created by lines 1–7.
problems.hint1: String literals 'hello' in (1) and (2) refer to the SAME String Pool object — 1 object total.
problems.hint2: new String('hello') in (3) creates a NEW heap object — different from the pool object.
problems.hint3: Integer.valueOf(200) creates a new Integer (beyond cache range -128 to 127). Integer.valueOf(100) uses cached — (6) and (7) are the SAME object.
problems.constraints: Assume standard JVM Integer cache range is -128 to 127.
problems.editorial: String Pool deduplication means literals sharing the same content share one object. new String() always creates a new object. Integer cache (-128 to 127): Integer.valueOf() returns the same cached object for values in range, new object for values outside.

problems.title: Iterative Fibonacci to Avoid Stack Overflow
problems.description: Recursive Fibonacci for large n causes StackOverflowError (exponential call depth). Implement iterative `fibonacci(int n)` that computes F(n) in O(n) time and O(1) space, safe for n up to 90.
problems.hint1: Keep only the previous two values: prev2=F(n-2), prev1=F(n-1), current=F(n).
problems.hint2: Loop from 2 to n, updating: current = prev1 + prev2; prev2 = prev1; prev1 = current.
problems.hint3: Use long — Fibonacci values get large quickly (F(92) > Long.MAX_VALUE).
problems.editorial: Iterative: uses O(1) stack space (1 stack frame total). Recursive: O(n) stack depth for linear recursion, O(2^n) for naive recursive Fibonacci. For any deep recursion, consider converting to iterative or memoized form.

problems.title: Object Reachability — Will It Be GC'd?
problems.description:
For each scenario, determine if the object becomes GC-eligible after the shown point:

A) `Object a = new Object(); a = null;` — after a=null
B) `List<Object> list = new ArrayList<>(); Object b = new Object(); list.add(b); b = null;` — after b=null
C) `static Object GLOBAL = new Object();` — during normal execution
D) `Object d = new Object(); WeakReference<Object> wr = new WeakReference<>(d); d = null;` — after d=null

Return String[] of 'GC-eligible' or 'Not GC-eligible' for A, B, C, D.
problems.hint1: An object is GC-eligible when NO strong references point to it.
problems.hint2: In B: list still holds a strong reference to the object even after b=null.
problems.hint3: WeakReference doesn't prevent GC — the object is eligible when only weak references remain.

testCases.output:
A: GC-eligible
B: Not GC-eligible
C: Not GC-eligible
D: GC-eligible

problems.editorial: GC traces from roots (stack, statics, JNI). A: no more references after null. B: list holds strong ref — not eligible. C: static = GC root = always reachable. D: WeakReference doesn't count as strong — when only weak refs remain, object is eligible.

problems.title: Fix the StackOverflowError — Power Function
problems.hint1: For negative exp: the current code calls power(base, -exp) which for exp=-3 gives power(base, 3) — that's fine.
problems.hint2: Wait — actually re-examine: power(2, Integer.MIN_VALUE). -Integer.MIN_VALUE overflows!
problems.hint3: Better fix: handle negative exp directly: return power(1/base, -exp) or return 1.0 / power(base, exp > 0 ? exp : -exp).
problems.editorial: The fix: for negative exponents, flip the base (1/base) and recurse with positive exp. Optionally use fast exponentiation (divide exp by 2 each step) for O(log n) depth instead of O(n).

problems.title: Find Max Recursion Depth
problems.description: Implement `findMaxRecursionDepth()` that determines the maximum safe recursion depth on the current JVM. Catch StackOverflowError when it occurs and return the depth reached. Run it and report the result.
problems.hint1: Recursive helper that increments a depth counter each call.
problems.hint2: Wrap the recursive call in try-catch StackOverflowError.
problems.hint3: Return depth when error is caught — that's the max depth.
problems.constraints: StackOverflowError is a Throwable — it can be caught.
problems.editorial: Each recursive call adds a frame. When stack is full, JVM throws StackOverflowError. Catching it in the deepest call returns that depth. Default -Xss on most JVMs gives 5,000-20,000 frames depending on frame size. Increase with -Xss2m for deeper recursion.

problems.title: StringBuilder vs String Performance
problems.description: Implement two versions of building a string from an array: `concatWithPlus(String[] parts)` using + operator, and `concatWithSB(String[] parts)` using StringBuilder. Measure and compare their performance for n=10000 parts. Return which is faster.
problems.hint1: String + creates a new object each iteration — O(n²) total bytes copied.
problems.hint2: StringBuilder.append() amortizes allocation — O(n) total.
problems.hint3: Use System.nanoTime() before and after each method call to measure.
problems.constraints: parts.length = 5000. Each part is a short string.
problems.editorial: String + in loop: each iteration creates and copies a new String. For n parts of avg length L: O(n²L) total byte copies. StringBuilder: internal char[] doubles on overflow (amortized O(1) per append), O(nL) total. At n=5000, the difference is dramatic: 100-1000x faster.

problems.title: Implement LRU Cache (Bounded Heap Use)
problems.description: Implement a `LRUCache<K,V>` with a maximum size. When the cache is full and a new entry is added, evict the least recently used entry. Use LinkedHashMap with access-order mode.
problems.hint1: Extend LinkedHashMap and override removeEldestEntry to evict when size > capacity.
problems.hint2: Pass accessOrder=true to LinkedHashMap constructor to track access order (not insertion order).
problems.hint3: get() and put() automatically update the access order — MRU moves to tail, LRU is at head.
problems.editorial: LinkedHashMap with accessOrder=true maintains nodes in access order (LRU at head, MRU at tail). removeEldestEntry is called after each put() — return true to evict. This is the standard Java LRU cache pattern, O(1) get/put. LeetCode #146 uses the same approach.

problems.title: Detect Memory Leak — Static Collection Growth
problems.hint1: Static list holds strong references to all registered listeners — GC cannot collect them.
problems.hint2: unregister() is empty — listeners are never removed even after they're 'done'.
problems.hint3: Fix: listeners.remove(l); in unregister. Or use WeakReference to allow GC.
problems.editorial: The leak: static List holds strong references to listeners. Empty unregister() means listeners accumulate forever. Fix: listeners.remove(l). Also copy the list before iterating (ConcurrentModificationException prevention). In real systems, use WeakReference<EventListener> so listeners can be GC'd if not held elsewhere.

problems.title: Merge Two Sorted Arrays In-Place
problems.description: Implement `merge(int[] nums1, int m, int[] nums2, int n)` that merges nums2 into nums1 in-place. nums1 has extra space at the end (total length m+n). Classic in-place merge using three pointers from the back.
problems.hint1: Place merged result from the end of nums1 backwards to avoid overwriting unread values.
problems.hint2: Three pointers: p1=m-1 (end of nums1 data), p2=n-1 (end of nums2), p=m+n-1 (write position).
problems.hint3: While both pointers valid: pick larger of nums1[p1] and nums2[p2], place at p.
problems.editorial: Fill from right to left — largest values first. This avoids overwriting nums1's unprocessed data. O(m+n) time, O(1) extra space. LeetCode #88. The in-place mutation works because nums1 has extra capacity at the end.

problems.title: Memory-Efficient Large Dataset Processing
problems.description: Process a large array of integers without loading all results into memory at once. Implement `streamingMax(int[][] chunks)` where chunks is an array of sub-arrays. Find the maximum value across all chunks using O(1) extra space (no extra array for all values).
problems.hint1: Keep a single `int maxSoFar` variable — never store all values.
problems.hint2: For each chunk, for each value: maxSoFar = Math.max(maxSoFar, value).
problems.hint3: Initialize maxSoFar = Integer.MIN_VALUE.
problems.constraints: At least one chunk. Each chunk is non-empty.
problems.editorial: Streaming processing: keep only the maximum seen so far. O(1) extra memory regardless of total data size. This pattern is essential for big data — imagine processing 1TB of data in 1MB chunks: the full dataset can't fit in heap, but a single int can.

problems.title: Stack-Based Iterative DFS
problems.description: Implement DFS on a graph iteratively using an explicit Stack<Integer> (avoiding recursive StackOverflow for very deep graphs). Count and return visited nodes from a start node.
problems.hint1: Push start node onto stack. While stack not empty: pop node, mark visited, push unvisited neighbors.
problems.hint2: Explicit stack on heap — can grow much larger than call stack.
problems.hint3: Use boolean[] visited to avoid revisiting nodes.
problems.editorial: Explicit stack on heap can hold millions of items (bounded by heap). Call stack limited to ~5000-20000 frames. For deep trees/graphs, always prefer iterative with explicit stack. ArrayDeque is faster than Stack (Stack is synchronized).

problems.title: Memory Pool — Pre-allocate Objects
problems.description: Implement a simple object pool for StringBuilder objects. Pre-allocate N StringBuilders at start. acquire() returns one from pool; release(sb) resets and returns it. Avoids repeated allocation/GC of StringBuilders in a high-throughput system.
problems.hint1: Pool is a Deque<StringBuilder>. Pre-fill with N empty StringBuilders.
problems.hint2: acquire(): pool.pollFirst() — return null if empty.
problems.hint3: release(sb): sb.setLength(0) to reset, then pool.addFirst(sb).
problems.editorial: Object pools reduce allocation/GC overhead by reusing objects. Key: reset the object state on release (sb.setLength(0)). Used in: Netty ByteBuf pool, database connection pools, thread pools. The heap cost is fixed (N objects), avoiding repeated GC of short-lived objects in hot paths.

problems.title: Trampoline — Tail Recursion Without Stack Overflow
problems.description:
Implement a trampoline to evaluate deeply-recursive functions without stack overflow. A trampoline repeatedly calls a thunk (lambda that returns either a result or the next thunk) until it gets a value.

Implement `trampoline(Supplier<Object> thunk)` and use it to compute factorial(100000) without StackOverflow.
problems.hint1: A Thunk is either a Supplier<Object> (next step) or a final value.
problems.hint2: Trampoline loop: while result is a Supplier, call it to get the next thunk.
problems.hint3: Encode as: return next thunk as a lambda; return final value as a BigInteger.
problems.constraints: n >= 0. Use BigInteger for large values.
problems.editorial: Trampoline converts recursion to iteration: instead of calling the next step, return it as a lambda. The loop calls it. Only ONE stack frame used at a time — deep recursion without StackOverflow. This is how Clojure, Scala implement tail call optimization in JVM. Java doesn't natively support TCO — trampoline is the workaround.

problems.title: Count Objects in Generations
problems.description: Explain with code why short-lived objects are cheap in Java's generational GC. Implement `shortLivedAllocation()` that creates and discards 1 million local objects within a loop body, and `longLivedAllocation()` that stores them in a list. Compare approximate GC impact.
problems.hint1: Short-lived objects (created and unreferenced within a loop iteration) stay in Young Gen — minor GC is fast.
problems.hint2: Long-lived objects promoted to Old Gen — full GC is expensive.
problems.hint3: Measure using Runtime.getRuntime().totalMemory() - freeMemory() before/after.
problems.editorial: Generational hypothesis: most objects die young. Young Gen (Eden + Survivor) is small and cheap to collect — minor GC < 1ms typical. Short-lived objects never leave Young Gen. Long-lived objects promoted to Old Gen (Tenured) — Full GC = stop-the-world pause. Design principle: allocate freely in hot paths (GC handles it) but don't retain objects longer than needed.

problems.title: Implement Stack Using Heap Array (Bounded)
problems.description: Implement a bounded `ArrayStack<T>` using a fixed-size array on the heap. Demonstrate push, pop, peek, isEmpty, isFull. The fixed size avoids unbounded heap growth.
problems.pattern: Heap Array as Stack Backing Store
problems.hint1: Use Object[] data = new Object[capacity] and an int top = -1.
problems.hint2: push: data[++top] = item. pop: return (T) data[top--]. peek: data[top].
problems.hint3: throw IllegalStateException when overflow or underflow.
problems.editorial: data[top--] = null is crucial: without it, the array holds references to popped objects — they can't be GC'd (logical leak). This is why ArrayList.remove() also sets the slot to null. Fixed-size stack guarantees O(1) amortized operations and bounded heap usage.

problems.title: Escape Analysis — Stack vs Heap Allocation Hint
problems.description: Write two methods: `doesEscape()` where a created object is returned (escapes — must be on heap), and `doesNotEscape()` where a created object is only used locally (JIT can stack-allocate via escape analysis). Use the JVM flag -XX:+PrintEscapeAnalysis (conceptual — explain in comments).
problems.hint1: Escape analysis: if an object doesn't escape the method, JVM can allocate it on the stack.
problems.hint2: An object 'escapes' if: returned, stored in a field, passed to an unknown method.
problems.hint3: doesNotEscape: create Point, compute distance, return double — Point never leaves the method.
problems.editorial: Escape analysis (JVM flag -XX:+DoEscapeAnalysis, default ON) detects if objects escape the method. Non-escaping objects can be stack-allocated (fast) or have their fields scalar-replaced (no object at all). This is why small value objects (Point, Rectangle) in math-heavy code are nearly free — JIT eliminates heap allocation entirely.

problems.title: Memory-Efficient Matrix Transpose In-Place
problems.description: Transpose a square matrix in-place. Only O(1) extra memory is allowed — no copying to a new matrix. Swap matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i] for i < j.
problems.hint1: Only iterate upper triangle: outer i from 0 to n-1, inner j from i+1 to n-1.
problems.hint2: Swap: int tmp = matrix[i][j]; matrix[i][j] = matrix[j][i]; matrix[j][i] = tmp;
problems.editorial: In-place transpose: swap upper and lower triangles. O(n²/2) swaps, O(1) extra space. Non-square transpose requires O(m*n) extra space — cannot be done truly in-place. This O(1) space principle applies broadly: prefer to mutate through existing references rather than allocating new structures.

problems.title: OutOfMemoryError — Heap Dump Analysis Simulation
problems.description: Write a program that deliberately causes OutOfMemoryError, catches it, and reports the heap state at the time of the error. Use Runtime to measure heap usage before and at OOM. Implement `causeOOM(int chunkSizeKB)` which allocates chunks until OOM.
problems.hint1: Allocate in a loop: list.add(new byte[chunkSizeKB * 1024]);
problems.hint2: Wrap in try { ... } catch (OutOfMemoryError e) { ... }
problems.hint3: Runtime.getRuntime().maxMemory() gives -Xmx value.

testCases.output: OOM after [N] MB allocated. Max heap: [M] MB

problems.constraints: Run with -Xmx64m for quick OOM. OutOfMemoryError is an Error — can be caught.
problems.editorial: OutOfMemoryError is a subclass of Error (not Exception) — but can be caught. After catching OOM, the JVM may be in an unstable state — only log and exit in production. Use -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError to automatically capture heap dump on OOM. Analyze with Eclipse MAT to find the largest object graph causing the leak.
